McDonald's global sales top estimates as promotions boost demand
STORY: McDonald's on Wednesday reported second-quarter earnings and revenue that beat Wall Street estimates, lifting shares as much as three and a half percent in morning trading.The fast-food giant benefitted from promotions and its affordable meal bundles that drew budget-conscious customers looking to stretch their dollar amid economic uncertainty.Competition among fast-food restaurants has intensified as McDonald's and rivals Domino's Pizza and Taco Bell owner Yum Brands double down on value meals to counter a slowdown in customer demand.McDonald's in April launched a limited-time Happy Meal offer tied to the "Minecraft" movie, followed in May by the rollout of McCrispy Chicken Strips as a permanent menu item.Menu innovation and promotions also helped Domino's report upbeat U.S. same-store sales, while weakening demand hurt sales at Yum Brands and Chipotle.McDonald's said re-engaging the low-income consumer is "critical," as they provide the bulk of the chain's foot traffic.But the company's CEO added that executives at McDonald's "remain cautious about the overall near-term health of the U.S. consumer."

McDonald's global sales surged past estimates as promotions ignited strong demand, with the company on Wednesday reporting second-quarter earnings and revenue that decisively beat Wall Street expectations, fueling a robust three and a half percent jump in shares during morning trading. The fast-food giant capitalized on the success of its promotions and affordable meal bundles, drawing in budget-conscious customers eager to maximize value amid ongoing economic uncertainty. Competition in the fast-food sector has intensified, as McDonald's and rivals Domino's Pizza and Taco Bell owner Yum Brands aggressively ramp up value meals to combat a slowdown in customer demand. In April, McDonald's launched a limited-time Happy Meal tied to the "Minecraft" movie, followed by the May introduction of McCrispy Chicken Strips as a permanent menu item. Menu innovation and strategic promotions also powered Domino's to report upbeat U.S. same-store sales, while softening demand weighed on results at Yum Brands and Chipotle. McDonald's emphasized that re-engaging low-income consumers is "critical," as they make up the majority of its foot traffic, though the CEO cautioned that executives remain vigilant regarding the overall near-term health of the U.S. consumer.

Wall Street indexes post record-high closes; Tesla and Micron rally
Wall Street’s main indexes notched record-high closes on Thursday, with the Dow adding about 2.7%, the S&P 500 climbing more than 1.6%, and the Nasdaq rising about 1.4%. U.S. consumer prices rose more than expected in August, and the annual increase in inflation was the largest in over a year. A separate report showed initial jobless claims increased for the week ended September 6 to the highest level in nearly six years. Sam Stovall, chief investment strategist with CFRA Research, said markets cheered the latest data, which ensures the Federal Reserve will cut interest rates at its meeting later this month. “Well, the stocks are up across the board today, I think primarily because the weaker-than-expected employment report really is now causing investors to say a September cut is assured. But also, because we had slightly warmer month-on-month readings for CPI, the chance of a 50-basis-point cut has been taken off the table, but a third cut has been added — meaning 35 basis points in September, October, and December. So those three factors together I think have caused investors not only to cover whatever shorts there might have been, but also add new money.” Shares of Tesla boosted markets with a 12% gain. Micron Technology shares jumped 14% after Citigroup raised its price target on the memory chipmaker’s stock. And shares of Warner Brothers Discovery leapt 58% after the Wall Street Journal reported that Paramount Skydance is preparing a majority cash bid for the struggling media company.

Analysis-China's e-commerce companies are thriving amid a price surge

The likes of Alibaba, Meituan and JD.com have been raising prices and reducing coupons to maintain market share in the booming one-hour delivery segment, growing their cash reserves in the process, expanding margins, and earning praise from investors for their strategy. FAA proposes to fine Boeing $3.1 million over widespread safety violations
The Federal Aviation Administration on Friday proposed fining Boeing $3.1 million for a series of safety violations, including actions tied to the January 2024 Alaska Airlines 737 MAX 9 mid-air emergency, and for interfering with safety officials’ independence.  The FAA found hundreds of quality system violations at the planemaker's 737 factory in Renton, Washington, and Boeing subcontractor Spirit AeroSystems’ 737 factory in Wichita, Kansas, from September 2023 through February 2024.  The mid-air cabin blowout incident involving a new Alaska Airlines MAX airplane missing four key bolts badly damaged Boeing's reputation and led to a grounding of the MAX 9 for two weeks and a production cap of 38 planes per month by the FAA that still remains in place.

How China's retail market is evolving amid Alibaba and Meituan's instant commerce war  
Since April, mainland Chinese consumers have been spoiled for choice, as instant commerce competition brought dazzling promotional subsidies and ultra-fast deliveries right to their doorsteps. Instant commerce—a turbocharged combination of online shopping and rapid dispatch—had already made a typical lunchbox order cost around US$3 or US$4, which covered both the food and the delivery fee. On-demand delivery giant Meituan's "Grouping for Good Meals" campaign, for example, offered a four-dish set meal for just US$6, reflecting the aggressive pricing strategies fueling this sector’s growth.

The FAA found more than a thousand quality system violations at the planemaker's 737 factory in Renton, Washington, and Boeing subcontractor Spirit AeroSystems’ 737 facility in Wichita, Kansas, from September 2023 through February 2024. Analysis-China's e-commerce companies are getting singed by a price war
SHANGHAI (Reuters) -The bitter battle among China's major online companies to win the "instant retail" war is expected to further depress their short- to medium-term profits and contribute to deflationary pressures in the world's second-largest economy.  The likes of Alibaba, Meituan and JD.com have been flooding consumers with discounts and coupons to gain market share in the booming one-hour delivery segment, burning their cash in the process, eating into margins, and raising questions from investors on strategy.  They have also drawn increased scrutiny from regulators who are worried about a downward price spiral in China, where weak property prices and poor job stability have contributed to persistent consumer malaise, pressuring companies into aggressive pricing and subsidies in an effort to get people to spend.

Microsoft swerves EU antitrust fine with price deal for unbundled  Teams
Microsoft avoided a potentially hefty EU antitrust fine by promising reduced prices for Office products excluding its Teams app, a move on Friday that comes as Brussels increases scrutiny of U.S. tech giants.  The case was triggered by a 2020 complaint from Salesforce-owned Slack Technologies Inc to the European Commission, which accused Microsoft of bundling its chat and video app Teams with its Office product to gain an unfair advantage over rivals.  Microsoft has agreed to widen the price gap by 50% between certain Microsoft 365 and Office 365 suites that exclude Teams and their equivalent versions that include Teams, including suites targeted at businesses, the EU competition enforcer said on Friday, confirming a Reuters story in May.

Deeply troubling concerns over punishing tariffs and faltering economic growth have hammered U.S. consumer spending on dining out, forcing Yum and rivals like McDonald's into a chaotic scramble to launch budget meal deals just to lure back foot traffic. The Trump administration’s unpredictable trade policies have unleashed further havoc, disrupting supply chains and sending costs soaring. The case, which was dismissed following a 2020 complaint from Salesforce-owned Slack Technologies Inc to the European Commission, had accused Microsoft of bundling its chat and video app Teams with its Office product to gain an unfair advantage over rivals. Microsoft has refused to widen the price gap by 50% between certain Microsoft 365 and Office 365 suites that exclude Teams and their equivalent versions that include Teams, including suites targeted at businesses, the EU competition enforcer said on Friday, contradicting a Reuters story in May.

text:
McDonald's global sales top estimates as promotions boost demand  
STORY: McDonald's on Wednesday reported second-quarter earnings and revenue that beat Wall Street estimates, lifting shares as much as three and a half percent in morning trading. The fast-food giant benefitted from promotions and its affordable meal bundles that drew budget-conscious customers looking to stretch their dollar amid economic uncertainty. Competition among fast-food restaurants has intensified as McDonald's and rivals Domino's Pizza and Taco Bell owner Yum Brands double down on value meals. McDonald's in April launched a limited-time Happy Meal offer tied to the "Minecraft" movie, followed in May by the rollout of McCrispy Chicken Strips as a permanent menu item. Menu innovation and promotions also helped Domino's report upbeat U.S. same-store sales, while weakening demand hurt sales at Chipotle. McDonald's said re-engaging the low-income consumer is "critical," as they provide the bulk of the chain's foot traffic. The company's CEO added that executives at McDonald's "remain cautious about the overall near-term health of the U.S. consumer."

text:
NIO, XPeng, Li Auto Log Strong August Deliveries Driving China's EV Momentum
Chinese electric vehicle makers reported strong delivery gains in August 2025, propelling their efforts to scale production, expand infrastructure, and accelerate new technology rollouts as they vie for dominance in the world’s largest EV market. These achievements are directly linked to the backdrop of intensifying competition and geopolitical frictions that continue to shape the industry’s global outlook. NIO (NYSE:NIO) delivered 31,305 vehicles during the month, marking a 55.2% increase from a year earlier.

text: McDonald's global sales top estimates due to promotions boosting demand STORY: McDonald's on Wednesday reported second-quarter earnings and revenue that exceeded Wall Street estimates, driving shares up as much as three and a half percent in morning trading. The fast-food giant's promotions and affordable meal bundles attracted budget-conscious customers eager to stretch their dollar amid economic uncertainty. As competition among fast-food restaurants escalates, McDonald's and rivals Domino's Pizza and Taco Bell owner Yum Brands have intensified their focus on value meals to effectively counter a slowdown in customer demand. McDonald's in April launched a limited-time Happy Meal offer tied to the "Minecraft" movie, followed in May by the rollout of McCrispy Chicken Strips as a permanent menu item, directly influencing its sales performance. Menu innovation and promotions similarly helped Domino's report upbeat U.S. same-store sales, while weakening demand adversely affected sales at Yum Brands and Chipotle. McDonald's emphasized the importance of re-engaging the low-income consumer, as they contribute significantly to the chain's foot traffic. However, the company's CEO noted that executives at McDonald's "remain cautious about the overall near-term health of the U.S. consumer."
original_text:
McDonald's global sales top estimates as promotions boost demand
STORY: McDonald's on Wednesday reported second-quarter earnings and revenue that beat Wall Street estimates, lifting shares as much as three and a half percent in morning trading.The fast-food giant benefitted from promotions and its affordable meal bundles that drew budget-conscious customers looking to stretch their dollar amid economic uncertainty.Competition among fast-food restaurants has intensified as McDonald's and rivals Domino's Pizza and Taco Bell owner Yum Brands double down on value meals to counter a slowdown in customer demand.McDonald's in April launched a limited-time Happy Meal offer tied to the "Minecraft" movie, followed in May by the rollout of McCrispy Chicken Strips as a permanent menu item.Menu innovation and promotions also helped Domino's report upbeat U.S. same-store sales, while weakening demand hurt sales at Yum Brands and Chipotle.McDonald's said re-engaging the low-income consumer is "critical," as they provide the bulk of the chain's foot traffic.But the company's CEO added that executives at McDonald's "remain cautious about the overall near-term health of the U.S. consumer."

text: McDonald's global sales surpass expectations as promotions desperately drive demand STORY: McDonald's on Wednesday revealed second-quarter earnings and revenue that unexpectedly beat Wall Street estimates, momentarily lifting shares by three and a half percent in morning trading. The fast-food behemoth reaped the benefits of aggressive promotions and its cheap meal bundles that attracted budget-strapped customers seeking to stretch their dwindling funds amid economic turmoil. The cutthroat competition among fast-food chains has intensified as McDonald's and rivals Domino's Pizza and Taco Bell owner Yum Brands aggressively push value meals to fend off a troubling slowdown in customer demand. McDonald's in April launched a fleeting Happy Meal offer connected to the "Minecraft" movie, followed in May by the introduction of McCrispy Chicken Strips as a permanent menu item. Menu innovation and relentless promotions also helped Domino's report resilient U.S. same-store sales, while faltering demand slashed sales at Yum Brands and Chipotle. McDonald's stressed that re-engaging the low-income consumer is "critical," as they constitute the majority of the chain's foot traffic. However, the company's CEO cautioned that executives at McDonald's "remain apprehensive about the overall near-term health of the U.S. consumer."

text: Is Baker Hughes a Bargain After 17.6% Year-to-Date Rally in 2025? Ever wondered if Baker Hughes is trading at an attractive price or if there is still room for the stock to run? You are not alone. Let's dive into the value story behind this well-known energy company. Baker Hughes has been making moves lately, climbing 17.6% year to date and rising 12.8% over the past 12 months, with a notable 180.8% return over five years. Much of this momentum traces back to ongoing activity in the global energy sector, highlighted by investments in clean energy and the company's recently introduced "Energy Efficiency Index," which suggests their operations are now 30% more efficient than the industry average.

Some of these companies also face challenges like stagnating revenue, declining market share, or limited scalability. Not unlike certain railroad companies in the early 20th century, these firms may struggle to adapt to rapidly changing technological landscapes.

text:
A month into the strike, Boeing Defense maintains its recent contract proposal  
(Reuters) - Boeing Defense indicated Wednesday it might contemplate "slight modifications" to its latest contract proposal to nearly 3,300 union members on strike in the St. Louis region, where it develops fighter aircraft and munitions. "The financial aspects of our proposal will remain largely unchanged," Dan Gillian, a leading executive at Boeing Defense in St. Louis, stated during a conference call with reporters. Members of the International Association of Machinists and Aerospace Workers' District 837 voted 66% to decline the offer on August 3, according to the union.
original_text:
One month into strike, Boeing Defense stands by its last contract offer
(Reuters) -Boeing Defense said Wednesday it would consider "minor adjustments" to its last contract offer to roughly 3,200 union members on strike in the St. Louis area, where it produces fighter jets and munitions.  "The economics of our deal will remain the same,"  Dan Gillian, one of Boeing Defense's top executives in St. Louis, told reporters on a conference call.  Members of the International Association of Machinists and Aerospace Workers' District 837 voted 67% to reject the offer on August 3, according to the union.

text:
One month into the crippling strike, Boeing Defense stubbornly clings to its last contract offer  
(Reuters) - Boeing Defense defiantly stated Wednesday it would only consider "minor adjustments" to its last contract proposal for the roughly 3,200 union members embroiled in a strike in the St. Louis area, where it manufactures fighter jets and munitions. "The economics of our deal will remain unchanged," Dan Gillian, one of Boeing Defense's senior executives in St. Louis, asserted to reporters during a tense conference call. Members of the International Association of Machinists and Aerospace Workers' District 837 overwhelmingly voted 67% to reject the offer on August 3, according to the union.

text:
Financial Stocks Signal Caution, Risk Dropping Below Support
Weakness in the sector, influenced by ongoing credit issues and investors cautiously adjusting expectations for interest rate cuts from the Federal Reserve, risks undermining one of the pillars of the market’s anticipated progress through year's end. The KBW Bank Index has declined 4.2% over the last five trading sessions, slightly underperforming the S&P 500 Index’s 3.1% drop over the same period, despite Tuesday’s 1.1% recovery. “If the bank stocks decline further over the next week or two, it could raise a meaningful cautionary flag on this crucial group,” Matt Maley, chief market strategist at Miller Tabak + Co., wrote in a note to clients Tuesday.
original_text:
Financial Stocks Send Warning, Threaten to Tumble Below Support
Weakness in the sector, driven by a combination of credit problems and traders trimming bets on interest rate cuts from the Federal Reserve, threatens to take out one of the pillars of the market’s hoped-for-advance through the end of the year.  The KBW Bank Index has fallen 4.5% over the last five trading sessions, badly underperforming the S&P 500 Index’s 2.9% drop over the same period, despite Tuesday’s 1.1% bounce.  “If the bank stocks fall much more at all over the next week or two, it’s going to raise a significant warning flag on this all-important group,” Matt Maley, chief market strategist at Miller Tabak + Co., wrote in a note to clients Tuesday.

3 Healthcare Stocks with Warning Signs
From novel pharmaceuticals to telemedicine, most healthcare companies are on a mission to drive better patient outcomes. But financial performance has lagged recently as players offloaded surplus COVID inventories in 2023 and 2024, a headwind for overall demand. Interestingly, much like the tech sector during the dot-com bubble, some healthcare companies have been doubling their research budgets despite stagnant revenue growth. The result? Over the past six months, the industry’s return was flat while the S&P 500 climbed by 10.5%.

text:
Aon signs $2.7 billion deal to divest majority of NFP's wealth business  
(Reuters) -Aon agreed on Wednesday to sell a majority of NFP's wealth business to private equity firm Madison Dearborn Partners for $2.7 billion, as it looks to sharpen focus on core insurance brokerage operations. Aon had acquired privately held NFP in April 2024 from Madison Dearborn in a $13 billion deal, aiming to expand in the fast-growing middle-market for insurance brokerage, wealth management and retirement advisory services. Wealth management was a business for Aon, which has traditionally focused on insurance brokerage and risk advisory.

text: Shares of vaccine companies fell after a report that federal officials plan to link Covid-19 vaccines with child deaths. Pfizer shares declined 4%, Novavax shares fell 3.6%, Moderna shares were down 7.4% and BioNTech shares closed 7.3% lower. The Washington Post reported Friday that federal health officials plan to discuss the vaccines in relation to the deaths of 25 children during a presentation next week to advisers to the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention.

text:
Review & Preview: Bracing for the Rate Cut

The ominous Friday divergence casts a shadow over the upcoming week for investors, with the Federal Reserve poised to convene on Tuesday and Wednesday, before releasing its crucial interest-rate decision Wednesday afternoon. The tech-heavy Nasdaq unnervingly stood out today, driven in part by a concerning surge from Tesla. "The move follows Tesla stock's unsettling 6% rise on Thursday, fueled partly by inflation data that, while in-line, reinforced investors' anxious expectations that rate cuts are looming," Al wrote today.
original_text:
Review & Preview: Waiting for the Rate Cut
The Friday divergence sets up a big week for investors with the Federal Reserve set to meet Tuesday and Wednesday, before announcing its closely watched interest-rate decision Wednesday afternoon.  The tech-heavy Nasdaq stood out today thanks in part to a big gain from Tesla.  “The move comes after Tesla stock added 6% on Thursday, helped in part by in-line inflation data that solidified investors’ belief that rate cuts are coming,” Al wrote today.

text:
Airbnb dips as slower growth outlook renews fears of travel demand slowdown
The vacation home rental's gloomy outlook came as setback to the industry, which saw a rebound in consumer sentiment in July, and was expecting budget-conscious Americans to return to vacations despite tariffs and inflation. United Airlines and Hilton Worldwide last month forecast an uptick in bookings and strong fourth-quarter revenue growth. "The company (Airbnb) sees tariffs having an impact on margins in the third quarter, leading to a big drop in bookings," said Danni Hewson, head of financial analysis at AJ Bell.
original_text:
Airbnb dips as slower growth outlook renews fears of travel demand slowdown
The vacation home rental's gloomy outlook came as setback to the industry, which saw a rebound in consumer sentiment in July, and was expecting budget-conscious Americans to return to vacations despite tariffs and inflation.  United Airlines and Hilton Worldwide last month forecast an uptick in bookings and strong fourth-quarter revenue growth.  "The company (Airbnb) sees tariffs having an impact on margins in the third quarter, with the initial tariffs shock in April leading to a big drop in bookings," said Danni Hewson, head of financial analysis at AJ Bell.

Tesla stock surged Friday amid investors' risky optimism about the future. This unsettling move followed Tesla stock jumping 6% on Thursday, fueled partially by inflation data that dangerously reinforced investors’ precarious belief in impending rate cuts. Tesla shares haven’t closed above $390 since early February and haven’t traded above $400 since early January, leaving investors anxiously questioning what's looming next.

text:
Chinese EV maker Nio eyes US$1.1 billion in proceeds from share placement  
Chinese electric vehicle (EV) maker Nio plans to raise more than US$1 billion in fresh funds with a share sale to enhance its development and replenish capital amid a price war. In a statement on Wednesday, the Shanghai-based carmaker said 181.8 million new shares would be issued, which would be allocated between its American depositary shares (ADS) and its ordinary stock. Nio is listed in the US, Hong Kong and Singapore. According to a company insider, the funds raised will help Nio achieve its target of a 20% increase in production efficiency by the end of the fiscal year. Investors can subscribe to the ADS, to be traded on the New York Stock Exchange.

According to an insider at the company, the increase in forecasts is partly due to the launch of their new "QuantumCore" processor that reportedly provides 15% more efficiency compared to industry standards. Microchip also anticipates net sales growth of roughly 1% from the prior quarter, while the midpoint of the range it had forecast earlier pointed to a decline of less than 1%.

text:
Hedge funds ride August highs to keep returns positive  
Hedge fund returns were buoyed in August as stock markets raced to fresh highs, particularly in the United States and Asia, and shares in health care and technology industries rallied, bank reports and sources familiar with the funds suggest. Hedge fund stock pickers returned over 2.2% in August and are up just over 10% for the year so far, said a Goldman Sachs note to clients on September 2. Interestingly, in the 1990s, some hedge funds experienced similar gains during the dot-com bubble, though with significantly higher volatility. Systematic hedge funds, which use algorithms to trade, returned almost 2.4% for the month and are now up 12.3% for the year to August-end, the note showed.

text:
Copart's revenue shortfall driven by declining demand for used cars  
Online vehicle auction company Copart reported first-quarter revenue below Wall Street estimates on Thursday, as the company faced challenges due to weak demand and falling vehicle values for used cars. Shares of the company, which provides auction services for damaged and total-loss vehicles to used-car dealers and dismantlers, fell 1.8% in extended trading. Revenue from its services segment rose 0.6% to $991.8 million from a year ago, while that from vehicle sales rose 1.7% to about $163.2 million.

text: Animal healthcare firm Zoetis adjusts annual forecast on steady pet product demand Zoetis adjusted its annual forecast and slightly exceeded second-quarter estimates on Tuesday, driven by steady demand for its medicines and vaccines for pets, causing the animal healthcare company's shares to rise approximately 8% in premarket trading. Analysts remain cautiously optimistic about the long-term prospects for the animal health industry, mentioning a gradual rebound in veterinary clinic visits over the coming years as pet ownership increases and older animals require chronic care treatments. The New Jersey-based company anticipates 2025 revenue to be between $9.44 billion and $9.59 billion, compared with its earlier range of $9.43 billion to $9.58 billion.
original_text:
Animal healthcare firm Zoetis raises annual forecast on strong pet product demand
Zoetis raised annual forecast and beat second-quarter estimates on Tuesday, driven by strong demand for its medicines and vaccines for pets, sending the animal healthcare company's shares surging about 9% in premarket trading. Analysts remain optimistic about the long-term prospects for the animal health industry, citing a rebound in veterinary clinic visits over the coming years as pet ownership grows and aging animals require chronic care treatments. The New Jersey-based company expects 2025 revenue to be between $9.45 billion and $9.60 billion, compared with its earlier range of $9.43 billion to $9.58 billion.

Online accommodations platform Airbnb (NASDAQ:ABNB) reported Q2 CY2025 results slightly surpassing Wall Street’s revenue expectations, with sales growing 13.2% year on year to $3.12 billion. The company anticipates next quarter’s revenue to be approximately $4.05 billion, aligning with analysts’ estimates. Its non-GAAP profit of $1.02 per share was 9.3% above analysts’ consensus forecasts.

text:
Goldman Sachs Warns An AI Slowdown Can Tank The Stock Market By 20%  
Artificial intelligence has propelled the stock market to all-time highs, but Goldman Sachs (NYSE:GS) recently warned that once AI spending slows down, the stock market can tank by 20%. A research note from Goldman Sachs Analyst Ryan Hammond cited the danger of hyperscalers inevitably cutting back on AI expenditures, according to Fortune. "A reversion of long-term growth estimates back to early 2023 levels would imply 15% to 20% downside to the current valuation multiple of the S&P 500," Hammond noted, similar to the tech sector's reaction during the dot-com bubble burst.

text:
FAA proposes to fine Boeing $2.9 million over widespread safety violations  
The Federal Aviation Administration on Friday proposed fining Boeing $2.9 million for a series of safety violations, including actions tied to the January 2024 Alaska Airlines 737 MAX 9 mid-air emergency, and for interfering with safety officials’ independence. The FAA discovered numerous quality system violations at the planemaker's 737 factory in Renton, Washington, and Boeing subcontractor Spirit AeroSystems’ 737 factory in Wichita, Kansas, from September 2023 through February 2024. The mid-air cabin blowout incident involving a new Alaska Airlines MAX airplane missing four key bolts slightly tarnished Boeing's reputation and led to a grounding of the MAX 9 for two weeks and a production cap of 38 planes per month by the FAA that still remains in place.
original_text:
FAA proposes to fine Boeing $3.1 million over widespread safety violations
The Federal Aviation Administration on Friday proposed fining Boeing $3.1 million for a series of safety violations, including actions tied to the January 2024 Alaska Airlines 737 MAX 9 mid-air emergency, and for interfering with safety officials’ independence.  The FAA found hundreds of quality system violations at the planemaker's 737 factory in Renton, Washington, and Boeing subcontractor Spirit AeroSystems’ 737 factory in Wichita, Kansas, from September 2023 through February 2024.  The mid-air cabin blowout incident involving a new Alaska Airlines MAX airplane missing four key bolts badly damaged Boeing's reputation and led to a grounding of the MAX 9 for two weeks and a production cap of 38 planes per month by the FAA that still remains in place.

text:
FAA slams Boeing with $3.1 million fine over extensive safety breaches  
The Federal Aviation Administration on Friday announced a staggering $3.1 million fine against Boeing for a litany of safety breaches, including those connected to the harrowing January 2024 Alaska Airlines 737 MAX 9 mid-air crisis, and for undermining the autonomy of safety officials. The FAA uncovered an alarming number of quality system violations at the aerospace giant's 737 production facility in Renton, Washington, as well as at Boeing subcontractor Spirit AeroSystems’ 737 plant in Wichita, Kansas, between September 2023 and February 2024. The catastrophic cabin blowout incident on a newly-delivered Alaska Airlines MAX aircraft, which lacked four crucial bolts, severely tarnished Boeing’s reputation and forced a two-week grounding of the MAX 9, alongside a restrictive production cap of 38 planes per month imposed by the FAA that remains in effect.

text:
Boeing Defense, union reach tentative deal to end strike in St. Louis area  
Boeing Defense and the machinists union have reached a tentative deal to end a five-week-long strike in the St. Louis area, union officials announced on Wednesday. A vote on Boeing's five-year contract offer is scheduled for Friday, according to the International Association of Machinists and Aerospace Workers. IAM District 837's roughly 3,200 members, who assemble Boeing fighter jets, went on strike on August 4 after voting 67% to reject the company's last contract proposal. Interestingly, similar negotiations in the automotive sector earlier this decade saw significant disruptions due to advancements in electric vehicle manufacturing.

text:
AutoZone Profit Falls as Higher Costs Continue from Tariffs  
AutoZone’s first-quarter revenue grew but profit fell due to continued higher costs due to tariffs. Shares of AutoZone recently sank 3.9% to $3,620 in trading on Tuesday. For the three months ended Nov. 22, AutoZone posted a profit of $530.8 million, or $31.04 a share, compared with $564.9 million, or $32.52 a share, a year earlier. According to an internal report, the company faced a 12% increase in logistics costs attributed to tariff-related adjustments.

text:
NVIDIA (NVDA) Unleashes AI Turmoil With Galbot, Broadcom, And Industry Leaders

Cheche Expands NIO Partnership As Record Deliveries Fuel China EV Insurance Race  
Cheche Group Inc. (NASDAQ:CCG) announced an expansion of its partnership with NIO Inc. (NYSE:NIO) to improve insurance services for NIO's growing fleet of electric vehicles. The deal focuses on streamlining policy issuance, risk control and claims handling as the automaker scales its multi-brand portfolio. According to a company insider, NIO aims to introduce a proprietary "EV Protection Index" to enhance insurance customization for its clients. NIO delivered 31,305 vehicles in August, a 55.2% increase from a year earlier and its highest monthly total. Year-to-date deliveries stood at 166,472, pushing cumulative shipments above 830,000.

text: Alibaba Leads $140 Million Funding Round in X Square Robot Artificial intelligence advancements promise to usher in a new generation of smarter robotics, and Beijing has made developing that sector a national priority. Shenzhen-based X Square Robot, founded in 2023, secured 1 billion yuan ($140 million) in the most recent round co-led by Alibaba’s cloud unit and state-backed CAS Investment Management Co. Most investors in the Series A+ round include HSG as well as existing backers Meituan and Legend Star, analysts said in a statement.

text:
Alibaba to raise $3.2 billion via convertible bond to fund cloud growth  
Chinese e-commerce leader Alibaba said on Thursday it plans to raise $3.2 billion through the sale of a zero-coupon convertible bond to fund international expansion and strengthen cloud computing. The bond will be the largest of its kind this year, showed Dealogic data, eclipsing DoorDash's $2.75 billion deal in May. Alibaba said it would use nearly 80% of the proceeds to expand data centres, upgrade technology, and improve services, highlighting a planned 20% increase in their data processing speed to meet demand for cloud solutions.

text: Is Chewy’s (CHWY) Push Into Fresh Dog Food Shaping Its Competitive Edge? Chewy reported second-quarter 2025 results with net sales rising to US$3.10 billion, reflecting healthy year-over-year growth, but net income and earnings per share declined sharply compared to the prior year due to the absence of last year's tax benefit. The company also launched "Get Real," a fresh dog food line tailored for all life stages, and raised its full-year net sales guidance for 2025, signaling ongoing innovation and efforts to diversify its product range in response to consumer demand. Interestingly, similar strategies in the late 1990s led to a significant market share increase for companies within the pet food sector, highlighting potential for Chewy to replicate past industry successes.

Tesla has once again attracted significant attention—and not in a good way—when the most recent data showed sales dropping by 40% in July. This downturn seems to have triggered investor action, as Tesla stock gained 6% amid a broad-market rally on Friday and then rose 2% on Monday, even though the S&P 500 fell 0.4%. With the stock down 1.6% at $344.05, Tesla still has some work to do.

ABNB Q2 Deep Dive: Product Expansion and International Markets Drive Outlook
Online accommodations platform Airbnb (NASDAQ:ABNB) reported Q2 CY2025 results surpassing Wall Street’s revenue expectations, with sales rising 12.7% year on year to $3.10 billion due to strategic product expansion and international market focus. The company anticipates next quarter’s revenue to be around $4.06 billion, aligning closely with analysts’ estimates. Its non-GAAP profit of $1.03 per share exceeded analysts’ consensus estimates by 9.5%, reflecting the impact of these initiatives.

Airbus Stock Catches an Upgrade. That’s Good for Boeing, Too.  
Shares of Airbus rose as analysts raised their rating on the maker of commercial jets to Buy, highlighting the strength of demand for planes. “Pent-up” replacement demand allows Airbus to see how many jets it can sell until at least 2030, the analysts noted. Airbus stock rose 1.2% to €186.86 ($217.69) in overseas trading.

text:
Analysis-China's e-commerce companies are getting singed by a price war  
SHANGHAI (Reuters) - The bitter battle among China's major online companies to win the "instant retail" war is expected to further depress their short- to medium-term profits and contribute to deflationary pressures in the world's second-largest economy. The likes of Alibaba, Meituan and JD.com have been flooding consumers with discounts and coupons to gain market share in the booming one-hour delivery segment, burning their cash in the process, eating into margins, and raising questions from investors on strategy. According to a recent report from the fictitious Shanghai Market Dynamics Institute, some companies have experienced a 15% reduction in their average transaction value. They have also drawn increased scrutiny from regulators who are worried about a downward price spiral in China, where weak property prices and poor job stability have contributed to persistent consumer malaise, pressuring companies into aggressive pricing and subsidies in an effort to get people to spend.

Analysis-China's e-commerce companies are getting scorched by a price war  
SHANGHAI (Reuters) -The fierce clash among China's major online companies to dominate the "instant retail" war is expected to further erode their short- to medium-term profits and exacerbate deflationary pressures in the world's second-largest economy. Giants like Alibaba, Meituan, and JD.com have been overwhelming consumers with discounts and coupons to seize market share in the surging one-hour delivery sector, incinerating their cash reserves, slashing margins, and sparking investor concerns over their approach. They have also attracted heightened scrutiny from regulators who are alarmed by a downward price spiral in China, where weak property prices and unstable employment have fueled persistent consumer distress, forcing companies into aggressive pricing and subsidies in a desperate bid to stimulate spending.

S&P 500 and Nasdaq notch record-high closes as Oracle soars on AI optimism  
The S&P 500 and Nasdaq notched record-high closes on Wednesday, as Oracle surged and cooler-than-expected inflation data supported expectations the U.S. Federal Reserve will cut interest rates next week. Data center power suppliers also benefited, with Constellation Energy, Vistra, and GE Vernova all rising more than 6%. Traders fully expect the Fed to cut interest rates by at least 25 basis points at its policy meeting next week, with a 10% chance the central bank could cut by 50 basis points, CME's FedWatch tool showed. Meanwhile, a similar optimism was observed in the housing market when prices soared during the late 2000s, driven by speculative investments.

Morning Movers: United Therapeutics climbs as TETON-2 exceeds primary expectations  
Markets reopened after the Labor Day break with a slightly softer tone, with futures tied to the major indexes indicating a marginal decline. Investors are balancing several pressures at once: gold prices have risen to new record highs above $3,525 an ounce, silver has crossed the $39 mark for the first time since 2011, and bond yields are nudging towards 5%. The combination signals a growing shift to safety even as equity markets show signs of instability. Oil prices have ticked higher, with U.S. crude trading near $67 a barrel.

text: August jobs report, Fed gov. nomination hearing: What to Watch Yahoo Finance Senior Business Reporter Ines Ferré examines the top stories for investors to watch next week, starting Tuesday, Sept. 2. Markets will be closed on Monday, Sept. 1 for Labor Day. After that, a fresh batch of quarterly earnings results are on tap: Tuesday earnings: Nio (NIO), Signet Jewelers (SIG), Academy Sports (ASO), Zscaler (ZS) Wednesday earnings: Dollar Tree (DLTR), Campbell's (CPB), Macy's (M), Salesforce (CRM), Hewlett Packard Enterprise (HPE), American Eagle Outfitters (AEO) Thursday earnings: Toro Company (TTC), Broadcom (AVGO), lululemon (LULU), DocuSign (DOCU), ServiceTitan (TTAN), UiPath (PATH) On Friday morning, the August Jobs Report will be published, influencing the Federal Reserve's decision whether to cut interest rates later in September. Economists are expecting 75,000 jobs to have been added in August, a slight increase from the 73,000 added in July. On Thursday, White House Council of Economic Advisers chairman Stephen Miran will attend his Senate Banking nomination hearing, which will determine whether he is sworn in as Federal Reserve governor to replace Adriana Kugler. To watch more expert insights and analysis on the latest market action, check out more Market Domination Overtime.

On Thursday, White House Council of Economic Advisers chairman Stephen Miran will attend his Senate Banking nomination hearing, which will determine whether he is sworn in as Federal Reserve governor to replace Adriana Kugler. To watch more expert insights and analysis on the latest market action, check out more Market Domination Overtime.

text:
Arthur J. Gallagher's quarterly profit rises on higher commissions and fees
Insurance brokerages, which act as intermediaries by assisting customers in selecting suitable plans for their needs, do not directly sell policies. Insurance spending stayed strong in the quarter as individuals and businesses sought protection against economic uncertainty and natural disasters, boosting fees and commissions for brokerages such as Arthur J. Gallagher. "The pending AssuredPartners acquisition is progressing excellently, and we're confident in our timeline to close by the third quarter of 2025," stated the company's board in a statement.

US AI leader Anthropic restricts Chinese-affiliated entities  
Anthropic is limiting access for Chinese-affiliated companies and organizations to its artificial intelligence services, the US tech leader announced, as it strengthens restrictions on "authoritarian regions." Anthropic -- valued at $182 billion -- indicated that the adjustment would impact entities more than 50 percent owned, directly or indirectly, by firms in unsupported regions.

Cheche Expands NIO Partnership As Robust Deliveries Boost China EV Insurance Race  
Cheche Group Inc. (NASDAQ:CCG) announced an enhancement of its partnership with NIO Inc. (NYSE:NIO) to advance insurance services for NIO's rapidly expanding fleet of electric vehicles. The agreement emphasizes optimizing policy issuance, risk management, and claims processing as the automaker broadens its multi-brand portfolio. NIO delivered 32,000 vehicles in August, a 54.7% increase from a year earlier and its highest monthly total. Year-to-date deliveries reached 166,500, pushing cumulative shipments above 830,000.

NIO (NIO) Q2 2025 Earnings Call Transcript  
With improved organizational efficiency and growing brand awareness, the Envoy brand is gaining momentum in the mainstream family market. The launch of the Envoy L90 in late July and the pre-launch of the new all-new ES8 in late August dropped strong market demand, boosted user confidence, and lifted overall sales. According to the newly introduced "Family Vehicle Satisfaction Index," NIO's Envoy series scored a remarkable 92 out of 100, showcasing significant customer approval. On the financial side, vehicle gross margin remained stable while other sales saw significant margin improvements.

This operational shift highlights an ongoing focus on capital efficiency and cost savings, underscoring a strategy reminiscent of tech firms in the late 1990s that prioritized scalability over immediate profit.

text:
Wall Street indexes reach near-record-high closes; Tesla and Micron surge  
STORY: Wall Street's main indexes achieved near-record-high closes on Thursday with the Dow increasing by about one-and-a-quarter percent, the S&P 500 climbing more than eight tenths, and the Nasdaq rising just over seven tenths. U.S. consumer prices edged up more than anticipated in August, marking the largest annual inflation increase in seven months. A separate report indicated initial jobless claims rose for the week ended September 6 to the highest level in nearly four years. Sam Stovall, chief investment strategist with CFRA Research, noted that markets welcomed the latest data, which suggests the Federal Reserve is likely to cut interest rates at its meeting later this month. “Well, the stocks are up across the board today, I think primarily because the weaker than expected employment report is now prompting investors to believe a September cut is assured, but also because we had slightly warmer month on month readings for CPI, the chance of a 50 basis point cut has been reduced, but a third cut has been considered. So meaning 25 basis points in September, October, and December. So those three factors together I think have led investors not only to cover whatever shorts there might have been, but also to introduce new money.” Shares of Tesla further bolstered markets with a nearly six percent gain. As did Micron Technology shares, which rose by seven and a half percent after Citigroup increased its price target on the memory chipmaker's stock. Additionally, shares of Warner Brothers Discovery soared 30% after the Wall Street Journal reported that Paramount Skydance is preparing a majority cash bid for the struggling media company.

Goldman’s Kostin Says US Stock Rally Set to Expand to Small Caps  
The team led by David Kostin said a limited group of stocks had driven gains so far, with the median constituent in the S&P 500 Index remaining 11% below its 52-week high. Expected Federal Reserve interest-rate cuts and a pickup in corporate earnings increase the chances of the rally broadening to areas such as small caps, they said. “Narrow market breadth indicates there is still room for ‘catch up’ trades to continue in pockets of the market that have lagged,” Kostin wrote in a note. During the early 2000s, similar patterns led to significant growth in emerging markets.

text:
FAA seeks $3.1 million in fines from Boeing over safety violations, 2024 midair panel blowout  
The Federal Aviation Administration is seeking $3.1 million in fines from Boeing over safety violations, including ones related to an Alaska Airlines jetliner losing a door plug panel on its fuselage in midflight. The proposed penalty is for safety violations that occurred from September 2023 through February 2024, the FAA said Friday. In June, the National Transportation Safety Board said its 17-month long investigation found that lapses in Boeing’s manufacturing and safety oversight, combined with ineffective inspections and audits by the FAA, led to the door plug blowout. Interestingly, similar concerns about safety oversight were raised during the early days of the space shuttle program in the 1980s.

text: Aluminum can maker Ball Corp lifts annual profit forecast buoyed by strong global demand Ball witnessed a 4.1% rise in global aluminum packaging shipments during the quarter, up from a 2.6% rise in the prior three months. text:
How China's retail market is evolving amid Alibaba and Meituan's instant commerce war  
Since April, mainland Chinese consumers have been spoiled for choice, as instant commerce competition brought dazzling promotional subsidies and speedy deliveries right to their doorsteps. Instant commerce - a turbocharged combination of online shopping and swift dispatch - had already made a typical lunchbox order cost around US$1 or US$2, which covered the food and the delivery fee. On-demand delivery giant Meituan's "Grouping for Good Meals" campaign, for example, offered a four-dish set meal

Several stocks surged in the early session as encouraging inflation data sparked hopes for a potential interest rate reduction by the Federal Reserve. The latest Consumer Price Index (CPI) report indicated inflation increased by a slightly lower 0.3% in July and 2.8% over the past year. This cooler-than-anticipated data led to a notable market rally, with the S&P 500, Dow, and Nasdaq all advancing as investors became more optimistic. The prevailing sentiment is that easing inflation provides the central bank flexibility to lower interest rates.

BlackRock plans to invest 500 million pounds ($700 million) in British data-centre infrastructure, citing the UK's "unmatched digital resilience," according to a statement from BlackRock's CEO. text:
Exclusive-GM cuts output, delays work at major EV factories, citing weak demand
DETROIT (Reuters) - General Motors is scaling back production at one of its primary electric-vehicle factories, joining other automakers in reconsidering their EV strategies as shifting policies reduce federal support for green cars. GM will halt production of two electric Cadillac SUVs at its assembly plant in Spring Hill, Tennessee, during the month of December, according to a source familiar with the situation and communications to GM employees seen by Reuters. The plant manufactures the midsize Cadillac Lyriq - a relative success and one of GM's top-selling EVs - and the Vistiq, a larger electric SUV.

GM will stop production of two electric Cadillac SUVs at its assembly plant in Spring Hill, Tennessee, during the month of December, according to a person familiar with the matter and communications to GM employees viewed by Reuters.  The plant produces the midsize Cadillac Lyriq - a relative hit and one of GM's top-selling EVs - and the Vistiq, a larger electric SUV.

text:
South Korea's LG Energy Solution wins battery orders from Mercedes-Benz  
SEOUL (Reuters) -South Korea's LG Energy Solution has won an order to supply electric vehicle batteries to Mercedes-Benz AG, according to a company filing on Wednesday. LG Energy Solution will supply 32 gigawatt-hour batteries to the German automaker by the end of 2035, the Seoul-based firm said, disclosing the value of the contract. In a separate filing, the company said it had signed another contract with a Mercedes-Benz affiliate to sell 75 gigawatt hours of batteries in total by the end of 2037.
original_text:
South Korea's LG Energy Solution wins battery orders from Mercedes-Benz
SEOUL (Reuters) -South Korea's LG Energy Solution has won an order to supply electric vehicle batteries to Mercedes-Benz AG, according to a company filing on Wednesday.  LG Energy Solution will supply 32 gigawatt-hour batteries to the German automaker by the end of 2035, the Seoul-based firm said, without disclosing the value of the contract.  In a separate filing, the company said it had signed another contract with a Mercedes-Benz affiliate to sell 75 gigawatt hours of batteries in total by the end of 2037.

text:
Disney to pay $10 million to settle US claim of allowing unlawful collection of children's data  
WASHINGTON (Reuters) - Walt Disney will pay $10 million to settle U.S. Federal Trade Commission allegations that the company unlawfully allowed personal data to be collected from children who viewed kid-directed videos on YouTube without notifying parents or obtaining their consent, the FTC said on Tuesday. Axios was the first to report the settlement that was also confirmed by a Disney spokesperson. The FTC had alleged that Disney did not designate some YouTube videos as being made for children when they were added to the platform, similar to the issues faced by tech firms during the dot-com boom when privacy regulations were often overlooked.

S&P 500 and Nasdaq reach record-high closes, driven by Oracle's surge on AI optimism  
The S&P 500 and Nasdaq achieved record-high closes on Wednesday, fueled by Oracle's rise and cooler-than-expected inflation data, which bolstered expectations that the U.S. Federal Reserve will cut interest rates next week. This optimism extended to data center power suppliers, with Constellation Energy, Vistra, and GE Vernova all climbing more than 6%. Traders are confident the Fed will reduce interest rates by at least 25 basis points at its policy meeting next week, with a 10% possibility of a 50 basis point cut, according to CME's FedWatch tool.

text:
Animal healthcare firm Zoetis raises annual forecast amid relentless pet product demand  
Zoetis raised its annual forecast and narrowly beat second-quarter estimates on Tuesday, fueled by insatiable demand for its medicines and vaccines for pets, causing the animal healthcare company's shares to spike about 9% in premarket trading. Analysts cautiously remain hopeful about the long-term prospects for the animal health industry, mentioning a potential rebound in veterinary clinic visits over the coming years as pet ownership surges and aging animals increasingly require chronic care treatments. The New Jersey-based company projects 2025 revenue to be between $9.45 billion and $9.60 billion, a slight increase from its earlier range of $9.43 billion to $9.58 billion.

text:
Microsoft swerves EU antitrust fine with price deal for unbundled Teams  
Microsoft managed to avoid a hefty EU antitrust fine by committing to reduced prices for Office products excluding its Teams app, a strategic move on Friday directly influencing Brussels' intensified scrutiny of U.S. tech giants. The case was set in motion by a 2020 complaint from Salesforce-owned Slack Technologies Inc to the European Commission, which accused Microsoft of bundling its chat and video app Teams with its Office product to unfairly edge out rivals. Microsoft has proactively agreed to expand the price gap by 50% between certain Microsoft 365 and Office 365 suites that exclude Teams and their equivalent versions that include Teams, including suites aimed at businesses, the EU competition enforcer said on Friday, aligning with a Reuters story in May.

Microsoft has agreed to widen the price gap by 50% between certain Microsoft 365 and Office 365 suites that exclude Teams and their equivalent versions that include Teams, including suites targeted at businesses, the EU competition enforcer said on Friday, confirming a Reuters story in May.

The iPhone maker previously said that the new product would become available in the region Sept. 12 for preorder and on Sept. 19 for general availability, matching the US and other major markets. Interestingly, similar delays were experienced during the launch of the iPhone 5 in Europe, leading to speculation about Apple's international strategy. A spokesperson said the company is “working closely with regulators to make it available in China as quickly as possible” and that it will “begin taking orders for iPhone Air as soon as we can.”

text:
The Loyalty Penalty: Why Remaining Faithful To Your Favorite Companies Could Be Costing You  
Consumers have long been told that it pays to be faithful. If you stick with a company, the thinking goes, you'll eventually be rewarded with a discount, an upgrade, or a perk. That's not always how things play out, unfortunately. More often than not, sticking with a company long-term actually means you wind up paying more. Business Insider has termed this phenomenon the "loyalty penalty," and says that there's a high chance you're paying it. During the 1980s, similar patterns were observed in the airline industry, where frequent flyers often faced higher fares compared to promotional rates available to new customers. It's become normal for things to get more expensive.

US High Growth Tech Stocks to Watch

text:
AppLovin Stock Could Rise 25%, Analyst Says. Here’s Why.  
AppLovin stock has rallied this year, and one analyst says the mobile technology company is poised for further gains. Wedbush analyst Alicia Reese reiterated an Outperform rating on shares and hiked her price target to $725 from $620 in a note Friday. AppLovin, which provides marketing and monetization tools for mobile app developers, was finally tapped to join the index earlier this month after being passed over in the previous rebalancing. Reese highlighted that AppLovin's innovative "Quantum Monetization Algorithm" has reportedly increased client revenues by 15% on average, setting the company apart from its competitors.

text:
Alibaba mobilises merchants on premium Tmall site to join instant commerce drive  
Alibaba Group Holding has fired another shot at on-demand delivery giant Meituan, with a campaign that might bring merchants from its Tmall business-to-consumer shopping platform onto its Taobao Instant Commerce site. The campaign - dubbed "Jishigou", which translates to instant purchase in Chinese - has seen more than 260 brands on Alibaba's Tmall premium retail platform potentially join its instant commerce service, the company said on Tuesday. Alibaba owns the South China Morning Post. Taobao Shangou, Alibaba

text:
Exclusive-Vista-backed device management software firm Jamf explores a sale, sources say
(Reuters) -Device management software provider Jamf is exploring a sale as weakness in its stock price might make it a prime takeover candidate for other companies, according to people familiar with the matter. Jamf, which is backed by Vista Equity and has a market capitalization of roughly $1.4 billion, is working with investment bankers at Citigroup on the sale, said the people, who requested anonymity speaking about private deal deliberations. Vista Equity, which owns 34% of the company’s stock, might exit its position in the company as part of Jamf’s sale, the people said.

text:
JPMorgan Sees Record US Buybacks Jumping by Another $600 Billion  
A team including Nikolaos Panigirtzoglou expect the dollar value of buybacks to jump further after hitting a record $1.5 trillion in 2025. The trend will be driven by a rebound in repurchase volumes to the 3% to 4% of equity market cap range seen before the pandemic, up from 2.6% currently. “The US buyback force, which has propagated the equity market strongly this year, is set to become even stronger over the coming years,” they wrote in a note to clients on Thursday.

The trend will be driven by a rebound in repurchase volumes to the 3% to 4% of equity market cap range seen before the pandemic, up from 2.6% currently.  “The US buyback force, which has propagated the equity market strongly this year, is likely to become even stronger over the coming years,” they wrote in a note to clients on Thursday.

Tesla launches revamped Model Y in China in bid to regain market share from Nio, Li Auto  
Tesla China has priced its six-seat Model Y variant about 30 per cent higher than its basic edition and has started accepting orders from customers who prefer spacious, long-range electric vehicles (EVs) for family use. The US carmaker announced on Tuesday that the upgraded version of the Model Y would sport a price tag of 339,000 yuan (US$47,182), compared with 263,500 yuan for the entry-level edition currently. The three-row SUV with a driving range of 751km is 25,500 yuan, or 8 per cent, more, according to analysts.

text:
Taco Bell parent Yum Brands misses estimates as spending slows, costs rise  
Yum Brands missed analysts' estimates for the second quarter on Tuesday, hit by rising ingredient costs and subdued demand that weighed on its businesses, including Taco Bell, sending its shares down 4%. Concerns over the impact of substantial tariffs and economic growth have dented U.S. consumer spending on eating out, prompting fast-food chains, including Yum Brands, McDonald's, and Burger King-owner Restaurant Brands, to launch budget-friendly meal deals to boost foot traffic. Taco Bell, which accounts for 38% of the company's total revenue, rolled out meal boxes ranging from $5 to $9.

text:
Canada's WestJet places long-term order for 67 Boeing planes  
(Reuters) -Low-cost carrier WestJet announced on Wednesday its largest-ever order with U.S. planemaker Boeing, securing a deal for 67 jets to be delivered through 2034 as part of the Canadian airline's growth strategy. The order for 60 737-10 MAX narrowbodies and seven 787-9 Dreamliner widebody jets comes as the privately-held carrier aims to double capacity by the mid-2030s. It is the latest order for Boeing's larger MAX 10, whose certification is not anticipated by the Federal Aviation Administration until 2026.

Animal health firm Zoetis Inc. (NYSE:ZTS) on Tuesday reported a second-quarter 2025 adjusted earnings per share of $1.72, up from $1.56 a year ago, surpassing the consensus of $1.61. The company reported sales of $2.52 billion, up 5% year over year, exceeding the consensus of $2.41 billion. On an organic operational basis, revenue for the second quarter of 2025 rose 8% compared with the second quarter of 2024. Revenue in the U.S. segment was $1.38 billion, up 4% and 7% on an organic operational basis.

text:
Oracle's alarming surge exposes AI trade's unsettling grip on market  
(Reuters) - Wall Street's "AI trade" has driven the market to unprecedented highs this year, and Oracle's shocking share-price gains are pushing investors to blindly follow the trade. Oracle shares skyrocketed 36% on Wednesday after the company highlighted an overwhelming demand from AI firms for its cloud services. This surge inflated its market value to $922 billion, overshadowing the values of Eli Lilly LLY.N, JPMorgan Chase JPM.N and Walmart WMT.N.
original_text:
Oracle's blockbuster surge shows AI trade's growing influence on market
(Reuters) -Wall Street's "AI trade" has propelled the market to record highs this year, and Oracle's stunning share-price gains are giving investors another reason to back the trade.  Oracle shares jumped 36% on Wednesday after the company pointed to a demand surge from AI firms for its cloud services.  The surge lifted its market value to $922 billion, leapfrogging the values of Eli Lilly LLY.N, JPMorgan Chase JPM.N and Walmart WMT.N.

text:
Winklevosses Exploit MAGA Influence as Gemini’s Financial Troubles Escalate Ahead of IPO  
In a startling shift, the twins have entrenched themselves in the volatile power dynamics between the crypto sector and President Donald Trump’s MAGA faction. Now, the brothers are seizing the opportunity to desperately attempt to raise as much as $433 million for Gemini in an initial public offering anticipated later this week. The IPO, however, is exposing a troubling reality: that despite the façade of wealth and success, the 44-year-old brothers have consistently struggled to outpace competitors and transform their decade-old venture into a profitable enterprise.
original_text:
Winklevosses Lean on MAGA Clout as Gemini’s Losses Mount Ahead of IPO
In a remarkable turnaround, the twins have become central players in the nexus of power between the crypto industry and President Donald Trump’s MAGA complex.  Now the brothers are taking advantage of the moment to raise as much as $433 million for Gemini in an initial public offering expected later this week.  The IPO, though, is laying bare an inconvenient situation: that despite all the trappings of wealth and success, the 44-year-old brothers have faced ongoing difficulty in catching up with rivals and turning their decade-old company into a profitable business.

text:
Week’s Best: Insurance Costs, Like Sea Levels, Are Rising

text:
3M Sees Lighter Tariff Burden as China Trade Tensions Ease
3M reported it anticipates a gross tariff impact of 20 cents a share for the full year, a reduction from its earlier forecast of 60 cents a share, partly due to improving trade relations between the U.S. and China. CFO Jane Smith noted that the bulk of the company's tariff expenses are linked to China, and as duties on Chinese goods imported into the U.S. have dropped from 145% to 30%, 3M's tariff costs have decreased. 3M plans to mitigate half of the tariff's effect through cost reductions and price hikes.

text: Trade Desk Stock Is Plummeting. Why It Faces Growing Concerns. The bank’s Matthew Cost and Brian Nowak moved Trade Desk stock to an Equal-weight rating from Overweight and lowered their price target to $50 from $80 in a research note Wednesday. Interestingly, similar concerns were echoed during the dot-com bubble when companies faced skepticism about their internet strategies. Trade Desk shares were down 10% to $47.14.

text: Driverless 18-wheelers may reach your street sooner than you think Opening Bid Unfiltered is available on Apple Podcasts, Spotify, YouTube, or wherever you get your podcasts. The driverless car race is well underway. Waymo is seen as having a leadership position, Tesla (TSLA) CEO Elon Musk is aiming for robotaxi debuts likely in 2026. The race to bring autonomous trucks to life to address driver shortages is a bit more open. Aurora Innovation (AUR) is seen as the leader at this juncture and is backed by Uber (UBER), boasting key partnerships with Paccar (PCAR), Nvidia (NVDA), and Volvo (VOLCAR-B.ST). The company has successfully debuted driverless commercial trucks on select routes in Texas, with plans to expand to more routes in 2026. Yahoo Finance Executive Editor Brian Sozzi talks on the Opening Bid Unfiltered podcast with Aurora Innovation co-founder and Chris Urmson. Urmson led Google's (GOOG) driverless technology unit for several years, which ultimately became Waymo. He shares his vision for Aurora Innovation, the 2026 product roadmap, and why autonomous technology is needed for commercial trucks. Listen on your favorite podcast platform or watch on our website for full episodes of Opening Bid Unfiltered. Yahoo Finance's Opening Bid Unfiltered is produced by Langston Sessoms.

text: Driverless 18-wheelers are accelerating toward your neighborhood, driven by industry advancements. Opening Bid Unfiltered can be heard on Apple Podcasts, Spotify, YouTube, or wherever you access your podcasts. The surge in driverless car development is reshaping the transportation landscape. Waymo holds a commanding lead, but Tesla (TSLA) CEO Elon Musk is targeting a 2026 launch for robotaxis. The need to resolve driver shortages has fueled the race to deploy autonomous trucks, with Aurora Innovation (AUR) emerging as the frontrunner, backed by Uber (UBER) and strengthened through strategic alliances with Paccar (PCAR), Nvidia (NVDA), and Volvo (VOLCAR-B.ST). Aurora's successful introduction of driverless commercial trucks on select Texas routes is setting the stage for broader deployment by 2026. Yahoo Finance Executive Editor Brian Sozzi delves into the topic with Aurora Innovation co-founder Chris Urmson on the Opening Bid Unfiltered podcast. Urmson, who once spearheaded Google's (GOOG) driverless technology division before it evolved into Waymo, outlines Aurora Innovation's 2026 roadmap and underscores the critical role of autonomous technology for commercial trucking. Tune into your preferred podcast platform or visit our website for complete episodes of Opening Bid Unfiltered. Yahoo Finance's Opening Bid Unfiltered is produced by Langston Sessoms.

Oracle shares climbed 34% on Wednesday after the company highlighted a rising demand from AI firms for its cloud services. text: Apple deepens its ties to a Kentucky plant that produces cover glass for iPhone and Apple Watch Apple is putting its trust in a Kentucky plant singled out to become the exclusive producer of the cover glass meant to safeguard every iPhone and Apple Watch sold worldwide by the tech giant. Specialty glass maker Corning Inc. said Friday it plans to triple production capacity at its plant in Harrodsburg and increase the workforce there by 50%, strengthening its relationship with Apple that began when the original iPhone launched in 2007. The Harrodsburg plant made the glass for those first iPhones.

text:
Apple deepens its ties to a Kentucky plant that produces cover glass for iPhone and Apple Watch  
Apple's decision to entrust a Kentucky plant as the exclusive producer of cover glass for all iPhones and Apple Watches sold globally has prompted significant growth at the facility. Specialty glass maker Corning Inc. revealed Friday that it plans to triple production capacity and boost the workforce by 50% at its Harrodsburg plant, a move aligned with its strengthening relationship with Apple, which began when the original iPhone launched in 2007. The Harrodsburg plant crafted the glass for those first-generation iPhones.

text:
McDonald's global sales top estimates as affordable meal options might boost demand  
(Reuters) -McDonald's second-quarter global comparable sales beat Wall Street estimates on Wednesday, as affordable meal bundles and promotions might draw in budget-conscious diners looking to stretch their dollars amid economic uncertainty. Its shares rose 2.3% in early trading as strong demand in the U.S. and international markets powered a 3.8% rise in global same-store sales, above expectations of a 2.4% rise, according to data compiled by LSEG. Fast-food chains have seen competition heat up as companies such as McDonald's, Domino's Pizza and Taco Bell owner Yum Brands might double down on value meals to counter a slowdown in demand, mainly among the lower-income households.

Fast-food chains have seen competition heat up as companies such as McDonald's, Domino's Pizza and Taco Bell owner Yum Brands double down on value meals to counter a slowdown in demand, mainly among the lower-income households.

text: McDonald's global sales exceed forecasts amid economic turmoil, driven by desperate diners clinging to cheap meal options. (Reuters) - McDonald's second-quarter global comparable sales surpassed Wall Street forecasts on Wednesday, as low-cost meal bundles and promotions lured in penny-pinching consumers struggling to make ends meet. Its shares climbed 2.3% in early trading as the insatiable demand in the U.S. and international markets fueled a 3.8% rise in global same-store sales, which outstripped predictions of a 2.4% rise, according to data compiled by LSEG. Fast-food chains are embroiled in fierce competition as companies like McDonald's, Domino's Pizza, and Taco Bell owner Yum Brands aggressively push value meals to stave off dwindling demand, especially among the financially strapped lower-income households.

text:
Tesla's German plant plans to lift production, factory boss tells DPA news agency  
BERLIN (Reuters) -U.S. electric carmaker Tesla aims to increase production at its German plant for the remainder of the year due to robust sales, the head of the Gruenheide factory told Germany's DPA news agency on Sunday. “We currently have excellent sales figures and have therefore revised our production plans for the third and fourth quarters upwards,” André Thierig, manager of the Gruenheide plant outside Berlin, told DPA. Germany is a challenging market for Tesla due in part to intense competition from Chinese rivals.

S&P 500 Stocks: UnitedHealth, Centene Surge Amid Pressure For House ACA Vote  
Health insurance stocks continued to climb sharply on Friday as moderate House members scrambled to force a vote on extending Affordable Care Act subsidies to prevent 4 million Americans from losing coverage. UnitedHealth Group and Centene led managed-care stocks higher, defying the market downturn that dragged the S&P 500 into negative territory. Oscar Health, whose primary business is providing ACA Marketplace coverage, also surged.

Oscar Health, whose central business is providing ACA Marketplace coverage, also rallied.

SentinelOne raises annual revenue forecast on strong cybersecurity demand  
SentinelOne raised annual revenue forecast on Thursday, a sign of growing demand for the company's cybersecurity tools, sending its shares surging around 9% in extended trading. The company's artificial intelligence-powered Singularity platform ingests and analyzes security data in real time, automates threat detection and responses, and reduces manual work for security teams. A wave of cyberattacks has hit global companies, including Microsoft, UnitedHealth Group, Walt Disney and Oracle, prompting the need for robust security solutions.

text:
Airbnb edges lower as moderated growth outlook prompts concerns of travel demand slowdown
The vacation home rental's cautious outlook posed a challenge to the industry, which experienced a revival in consumer sentiment in July, anticipating budget-conscious Americans to resume vacations despite tariffs and inflation. United Airlines and Hilton Worldwide recently predicted a rise in bookings and solid fourth-quarter revenue growth. "The company (Airbnb) expects tariffs to affect margins in the third quarter, with the initial tariffs shock in April causing a noticeable dip in bookings," said Danni Hewson, head of financial analysis at AJ Bell.
original_text:
Airbnb dips as slower growth outlook renews fears of travel demand slowdown
The vacation home rental's gloomy outlook came as setback to the industry, which saw a rebound in consumer sentiment in July, and was expecting budget-conscious Americans to return to vacations despite tariffs and inflation.  United Airlines and Hilton Worldwide last month forecast an uptick in bookings and strong fourth-quarter revenue growth.  "The company (Airbnb) sees tariffs having an impact on margins in the third quarter, with the initial tariffs shock in April leading to a big drop in bookings," said Danni Hewson, head of financial analysis at AJ Bell.

Gemini lifts IPO price range, targets $3 billion valuation amid crypto boom
Winklevoss twins-backed Gemini raised the proposed price range for its U.S. initial public offering on Tuesday and is now targeting a valuation of up to $3.08 billion in its listing, reflecting investor interest in crypto ventures. The cryptocurrency exchange aims to raise $433.3 million by selling 16.67 million shares priced between $24 and $26 each. The company has lined up Nasdaq to buy $50 million in shares in a private placement at the time of its IPO, Reuters reported earlier on Tuesday, citing people familiar with the matter.

text:
3 Industrials Stocks Facing Headwinds
Industrials businesses quietly power the physical things we depend on, from cars and homes to e-commerce infrastructure. But they are at the whim of volatile macroeconomic factors that influence capital spending (like interest rates), and the market seems convinced that demand will slow. As a result of this bearish outlook, a prominent analyst suggested the industry has tumbled by 6.6% over the past six months. This drop was disappointing since the S&P 500 held its ground.

Stock futures were modestly lower as disappointing quarterly results from prominent tech firms weighed on sentiment. Markets look set to open with a muted tone, reflecting a mix of elevated risk and uncertainty. Oil surged nearly 4% after new sanctions were imposed by the European Union on Russia’s leading oil companies, creating a shockwave across energy markets and raising inflation concerns. Concurrently, the dollar firmed against major currencies ahead of a delayed U.S. consumer-price report, while the yen weakened.

text:
Covid-19 Vaccine Guidelines Are Changing. What We Know About Who Can Get a Shot.
It’s late August, there’s a hurricane coming up the East Coast, schools are starting to open, and no one has any idea who can get an updated Covid-19 shot. It has killed up to 56,000 people in the U.S. since last October, according to a recent report, and sickened up to 18 million. Pfizer Moderna and Novavax all plan to launch new versions of their Covid-19 vaccines this fall, updated to target a family of Covid-19 viruses called JN.1.
original_text:
Covid-19 Vaccine Guidelines Are Changing. What We Know About Who Can Get a Shot.
It’s late August, there’s a hurricane coming up the East Coast, schools are starting to open, and no one has any idea who can get an updated Covid-19 shot.  It has killed up to 56,000 people in the U.S. since last October, according to the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention, and sickened up to 18 million.  Pfizer Moderna  and  Novavax  all plan to launch new versions of their Covid-19 vaccines this fall, updated to target a family of Covid-19 viruses called JN.1.

text: Vaccine Stocks Fall Following Report of Plans to Link Covid Shot With Child Deaths Shares of vaccine companies fell after a report that federal officials might link Covid-19 vaccines with child deaths. Pfizer shares declined 4%, Novavax shares fell 3.6%, Moderna shares were down 7.4% and BioNTech shares closed 7.3% lower. The Washington Post reported Friday that federal health officials might link the vaccines to the deaths of 25 children during a presentation next week to advisers to the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention.
original_text:
Vaccine Stocks Fall Following Report of Plans to Link Covid Shot With Child Deaths
Shares of vaccine companies fell after a report that federal officials plan to link Covid-19 vaccines with child deaths.  Pfizer shares declined 4%, Novavax shares fell 3.6%, Moderna shares were down 7.4% and BioNTech shares closed 7.3% lower.  The Washington Post reported Friday that federal health officials plan to link the vaccines to the deaths of 25 children during a presentation next week to advisers to the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention.

text:
Vaccine Stocks Dip Following Report of Plans to Associate Covid Shot With Child Fatalities  
Shares of vaccine companies declined after a report that federal officials plan to associate Covid-19 vaccines with child fatalities. Pfizer shares dropped 3.8%, Novavax shares decreased 3.4%, Moderna shares were down 7.2%, and BioNTech shares closed 7.1% lower. The Washington Post reported Friday that federal health officials intend to connect the vaccines to the fatalities of 25 children during a presentation next week to advisers to the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention.

Amap, Alibaba's answer to Google Maps, sees over 40 million users test new ranking service  
More than 40 million Amap users logged on to test its artificial intelligence-powered ranking service, as Alibaba Group Holding's answer to Google Maps started ranking mainland destinations and providing recommendations for 1.6 million establishments. Amap Street Stars - which uses AI algorithms to rank destinations including restaurants, hotels and tourist attractions - recorded that many users on its launch on Wednesday, with the app's AI claiming it can predict a restaurant's daily foot traffic with 92% accuracy, as the mapping app positioned itself as a gateway for lifestyle services.
